{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":37,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"hairdresser","city_of_living":"Rome","employment_status":"self-employed","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"divorced","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Judaism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["football (calcio)","yoga","classical music","running","birdwatching"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":56,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":9,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":53,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":48,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":95,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"Living in Rome as a hairdresser, I often find myself scrolling through Instagram or Facebook just to catch up on the latest trends for my clients, but I also have to be wary of the flood of misinformation that can slip through the cracks. The first thing I do, because I don’t want to waste time and energy, is to keep a mental checklist of red flags: sensational headlines, lack of credible sources, and too many emojis. When something feels off, I pause and check the profile that posted it; a verified badge or a history of reliable content can give me some reassurance. If the claim is about a health product or a hair treatment, I cross-reference it with reputable websites like the European Medicines Agency or the Italian Ministry of Health, because I know that misinformation can quickly turn into a health risk for my clients.
I also use the built-in tools on the platforms themselves: reporting fake accounts, flagging questionable posts, and turning on the fact-checking overlays that some social media sites now offer. Even though I am not a tech expert, these features are surprisingly user-friendly and help keep my feed cleaner. When I see a post that could be harmful, I sometimes write a short comment with a fact-checked source, because I believe that a little bit of correct information can counterbalance a single piece of misinformation.
Because I have a family, I am also careful about how often I visit social media; the anxiety that comes with constantly seeing false claims can be draining, so I limit my time to a few short sessions each week. I keep my phone on silent during work hours, which helps me concentrate on cutting hair, and I set a reminder to check the news only at a certain time each day. This routine keeps me from being overwhelmed while still staying informed.
In the end, it is about balance: staying alert enough to spot fake content, yet not letting it consume my mental energy. By using simple tools, cross-referencing reputable sources, and setting limits on my usage, I can protect my own sanity and the well-being of my clients.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Living in Rome as a hairdresser, I often find myself scrolling through Instagram or Facebook just to catch up on the latest trends for my clients, but I also have to be wary of the flood of misinformation that can slip through the cracks. The first thing I do, because I don’t want to waste time and energy, is to keep a mental checklist of red flags: sensational headlines, lack of credible sources, and too many emojis. When something feels off, I pause and check the profile that posted it; a verified badge or a history of reliable content can give me some reassurance. If the claim is about a health product or a hair treatment, I cross-reference it with reputable websites like the European Medicines Agency or the Italian Ministry of Health, because I know that misinformation can quickly turn into a health risk for my clients.
I also use the built-in tools on the platforms themselves: reporting fake accounts, flagging questionable posts, and turning on the fact-checking overlays that some social media sites now offer. Even though I am not a tech expert, these features are surprisingly user-friendly and help keep my feed cleaner. When I see a post that could be harmful, I sometimes write a short comment with a fact-checked source, because I believe that a little bit of correct information can counterbalance a single piece of misinformation.
Because I have a family, I am also careful about how often I visit social media; the anxiety that comes with constantly seeing false claims can be draining, so I limit my time to a few short sessions each week. I keep my phone on silent during work hours, which helps me concentrate on cutting hair, and I set a reminder to check the news only at a certain time each day. This routine keeps me from being overwhelmed while still staying informed.
In the end, it is about balance: staying alert enough to spot fake content, yet not letting it consume my mental energy. By using simple tools, cross-referencing reputable sources, and setting limits on my usage, I can protect my own sanity and the well-being of my clients.
